question: Why is the concept of a sampling distribution fundamental to statistical inference and machine learning?
expectedPoints: The sampling distribution describes the variability of a statistic (like the mean) across many samples from the same population.
It allows us to quantify the uncertainty and precision of our estimates, forming the basis for confidence intervals.
It is the key theoretical foundation that justifies methods like hypothesis testing, where we assess how extreme a observed statistic is under a null model.

question: Explain the Central Limit Theorem (CLT) and its critical role in defining the sampling distribution for the mean.
expectedPoints: The CLT states that the sampling distribution of the sample mean will approach a normal distribution as the sample size increases, regardless of the population's original distribution (given finite variance).
This allows us to use the properties of the normal distribution (e.g., the empirical rule) to make probabilistic statements about the sample mean.
It justifies the use of Gaussian-based inference (e.g., z-tests, t-tests) for the mean even when the underlying data is not Gaussian, provided the sample size is sufficiently large.

question: Distinguish between the standard deviation of a population (σ) and the standard error of the mean (SEM).
expectedPoints: The population standard deviation (σ) measures the spread or variability of individual data points within the entire population.
The standard error of the mean (SEM) measures the spread or variability of the sample mean statistic across different possible samples. It is calculated as σ/√n.
The SEM decreases as the sample size (n) increases, reflecting that larger samples lead to more precise estimates of the population mean.
